item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, +1. But could have had more of a "punchline". Like, "Oh damn, Christina Mattingly is the reason we have Mitochondria", "But wait, where did she get the Mitochondria". DUN DUN DUNNNNNNNN!

(Or something).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any article that gets me excited about its implications and wanting more is nice. One that does that while feeling complete is even nicer. One that does both of those in so few words is a treat.

+1, I wonder what effect Christina’s remains had on Earth’s biological development

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this, thank you for the suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The objects have to be on the same trajectory in the latter stage of the fall through all of Earth's atmosphere, not vacuum, which is extremely unlikely.

I agree with the second assumption being reasonable in the real world, but not when a) I maintain the chances of the two things coming into contact are tiny and b) this is the Foundation universe when the Foundation knows there are all sorts of other space aliens and time travellers and whatever. Perhaps leaving it as 'trace amounts of biological matter from an unidentified multi-cellular organism were found' would at least sidestep that problem and leave some mystery for the reader to fill in the blanks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two objects falling through a near-vacuum from the same starting position towards the surface of a planet have a decent chance of eventually coming into contact with one another. Especially if one of those things is biological and would tend to, shall we say, flatten out a bit on impact.

You are correct that it is completely infeasible that fossil records would be able to definitely identify a human. However, it would potentially be sufficient to suggest that such a fossil was formed by a multi-cellular organism, at a point in time in which no multi-cellular life is suspected to exist on the planet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty neat, but I don't know how you would have been able to identify 4 billion year old biological traces as the remains of a person, or how dropping a person into space and a time capsule into space would have the two items collide with each other, even if it was in the same small region. Unless there's something these have to do with the SCP I'm missing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to go with a strong novote on this. While I can enjoy short SCPs, I do believe there is such thing as too short. However, the fridge logic involved with this one was too good not to upvote. So you got me!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't get the appeal of this. I honestly just thought this was a newbie's coldpost before I saw who wrote it. It feels unfinished.

Okay, I think I understand what you're implying a little better now. Still, took a little longer than it should have for me to get it, so no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a lot of potential, but for now it just exists (which is still compelling) which makes it less than it could be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
claiming that his wife, Christina Mattingly, had "fallen into space in the bathtub".

That's a great line, truly. Simple, bizarre.

I don't feel that the time capsule stuff is very interesting, though, and kind of ends the entry on a bit of a much less punchy note (and it just reiterates that yup, she fell through). I don't know what the best way to end this article is, but I don't think this is it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you could describe the contents of the bathtub more and explain how the aperture looks from within, it would make the article more interesting.

I feel like that there is more to this thing other than being a portal to a different era. There could be a lot more done with this.

Otherwise, novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This came around because of a really cool pic a friend took of a bath bomb.

Sincere thanks to RockTeethMothEyes and magnadeus for crit.
